summary: Gruber revisits NYT columnist Joe Nocera, who previously criticized the iPhone's non-replaceable battery, and now finds him complaining bitterly about his buggy, crashy Palm Treo. Nocera dismisses all smartphones as inadequate, calling the iPhone only good for 'music and media' while ignoring its revolutionary web browser. Gruber argues this characterization is forced and contrary to nearly all reviews, particularly the omission of Safari's mobile browsing superiority. He draws a parallel between dismissing the iPhone as a media-only device and the old canard that Macs were only good for graphics. Gruber concludes that Nocera sounds like someone who would love an iPhone but refuses to consider it.
key_points: Nocera's Palm Treo is plagued with crashes, random reboots, and terrible web access, yet he still won't consider the iPhone
Nocera's dismissal of the iPhone as only good for 'music and media' conspicuously ignores its class-leading web browser
Describing the iPhone's phone features as 'lousy' contradicts the consensus of nearly all reviews
The pattern of dismissing iPhones as single-purpose devices mirrors the old myth that Macs were only good for graphics
money_quotes: I'll concede that the iPhone email app could be improved; it's probably the app for which I have the most feature requests.
But it seems forced — and contrary to nearly all reviews — to describe the iPhone's phone features as 'lousy'.
It's rather conspicuous that Nocera neglects to mention the iPhone's web browser, which kicks any other mobile device's ass.
There's a lot to criticize about the iPhone, but overall, it's leagues better than the Treo or Q, and better than BlackBerrys for everything other than email.
iPhones are only good for music and video is the new Macs are only good for graphics and design.
Read the whole column and it seems like Nocera would love an iPhone, but that he's not even willing to consider it. His loss.
key_insight: Dismissing the iPhone as only good for media while suffering through a terrible Treo is the smartphone-era equivalent of the old 'Macs are only for graphics' myth — a bias that ignores the evidence in front of you.
